participants.extracted: Eleven consecutive adult patients (age ≥18 years) with homozygous sickle cell anemia (HbSS) or HbSβ0-thalassemia, confirmed by HPLC, were enrolled from the Academic Medical Center (Amsterdam, The Netherlands); 10 had HbSS and 1 had HbS-β0-thalassemia, with a median age of 23 years (range 20–47), and 6 were male and 5 female. All had sickle cell disease and were outpatients. Exclusion criteria included recent painful crisis or blood transfusion, pregnancy or desire for pregnancy, reduced renal function, peptic ulcer disease, active infection, autoimmune inflammatory disease, and use of hydroxyurea or anticoagulants. Ten race- and age-matched healthy volunteers were also included as controls for baseline comparisons.

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized to oral N-acetylcysteine (NAC) 1,200 mg/day or 2,400 mg/day, given as 600 mg tablets dissolved in water and taken twice daily for 6 weeks, followed by 6 weeks of follow-up after NAC cessation.

outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: reduction of erythrocyte phosphatidylserine (PS) expression, measured by flow cytometry as percentage of erythrocytes with external PS exposure, assessed at baseline and during treatment follow-up (every 2 weeks during the 6-week NAC treatment period) and again 6 weeks after NAC cessation. Secondary outcomes: markers of hemolysis including hemoglobin concentration, reticulocyte count, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and bilirubin (total and direct), measured from blood samples at baseline, every 2 weeks during treatment, and 6 weeks after cessation; hypercoagulability markers including prothrombin fragment 1+2 (F1+2), thrombin-antithrombin (TAT) complexes, and thrombin generation parameters (lag time, peak value, endogenous thrombin potential), measured in plasma at baseline, during treatment follow-up, and after cessation; endothelial activation markers including soluble vascular adhesion molecule-1 (sVCAM-1) and von Willebrand factor antigen (vWF:Ag), measured in serum/plasma on the same schedule; nitric oxide bioavailability markers including l-arginine, asymmetric dimethylarginine (ADMA), symmetric dimethylarginine (SDMA), and NT-proBNP, measured in plasma at baseline and follow-up visits; inflammation measured by ultra-sensitive C-reactive protein (US-CRP), measured in plasma at baseline and follow-up; glutathione status including total glutathione, reduced glutathione (GSH), oxidized glutathione (GSSG), and GSH/GSSG ratio, measured in whole blood/erythrocytes at baseline, during treatment, and after cessation; cell-free hemoglobin measured spectrophotometrically in plasma at baseline, during treatment, and after cessation; advanced glycation end products including pentosidine and Nε-(carboxy-methyl)lysine (CML), measured in plasma at baseline and after 6 weeks of NAC treatment; tolerability/adverse events including gastrointestinal complaints and other side effects, assessed by questionnaires at each 2-week follow-up visit throughout treatment and post-treatment follow-up; daily pain scores (visual analogue scale) recorded in a patient diary throughout the study period; and SCD-related hospital admission/acute vaso-occlusive pain crisis as a clinical event monitored during the 12-week study period.
